structures.preferredName: Frontal horn of the lateral ventricle
acceptedAnswers: frontal horn of the lateral ventricle
structures.description: CSF-containing anterior portion of each lateral ventricle, bordered laterally by the caudate head and medially by the septum pellucidum.
clinicalNotes: Helpful landmark for caudate head and corpus callosum orientation on axial T1 images.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the CSF space highlighted anteriorly between the caudate heads.
quizTargets.hint: It is the anterior part of the lateral ventricle.

structures.description: Midline CSF cavity of the diencephalon, located between the thalami and communicating with the lateral ventricles through the foramina of Monro.
clinicalNotes: Expansion can be seen in hydrocephalus and central volume loss.

quizTargets.prompt: Name the midline CSF space between the thalami.
quizTargets.hint: It sits inferior to the bodies of the lateral ventricles.

structures.description: Anterior component of the caudate nucleus, forming the lateral wall of the frontal horn of the lateral ventricle.
clinicalNotes: Atrophy can contribute to enlargement of the frontal horns, classically discussed in Huntington disease.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the gray matter structure lateral to the frontal horn.
quizTargets.hint: It is part of the striatum.

structures.description: Lateral component of the lentiform nucleus, separated from the caudate and thalamus by the internal capsule.
clinicalNotes: A common landmark in basal ganglia hemorrhage and movement disorder anatomy.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the lateral portion of the lentiform nucleus.
quizTargets.hint: It lies lateral to the globus pallidus.

structures.description: Paired deep gray matter nuclei flanking the third ventricle and forming a major relay hub for cortical pathways.
clinicalNotes: Vascular lesions may involve thalamoperforator or posterior circulation territories depending on location.

quizTargets.prompt: Name the deep gray structure bordering the third ventricle.
quizTargets.hint: It is a major relay nucleus.

structures.preferredName: Posterior limb of the internal capsule
acceptedAnswers: posterior limb of the internal capsule
structures.description: Compact white matter pathway between the thalamus medially and lentiform nucleus laterally.
clinicalNotes: Contains corticospinal fibers; small infarcts here can cause dense contralateral motor deficits.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the white matter band between thalamus and lentiform nucleus.
quizTargets.hint: Motor tract fibers are packed here.

structures.description: Anterior bend of the corpus callosum connecting frontal lobe white matter across the midline.
clinicalNotes: Useful landmark for frontal horns and anterior commissure region.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the anterior bend of the corpus callosum.
quizTargets.hint: It arches over the frontal horns.

structures.description: Medial temporal lobe structure involved in memory, located along the floor of the temporal horn.
clinicalNotes: Assess symmetry and volume in mesial temporal sclerosis and neurodegenerative disease contexts.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the medial temporal lobe structure along the floor of the temporal horn.
quizTargets.hint: It is central to memory circuitry.

structures.description: Middle portion of the brainstem, located anterior to the fourth ventricle and between the midbrain and medulla.
clinicalNotes: Pontine lesions may affect corticospinal tracts, cranial nerve nuclei, and transverse pontocerebellar fibers.

quizTargets.prompt: Name the rounded middle segment of the brainstem.
quizTargets.hint: It lies anterior to the fourth ventricle.

structures.description: CSF cavity posterior to the pons and medulla and anterior to the cerebellum.
clinicalNotes: Important compartment in obstructive hydrocephalus and posterior fossa mass effect.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the CSF space between brainstem and cerebellum.
quizTargets.hint: It communicates superiorly with the cerebral aqueduct.

structures.description: Opening in the greater wing of the sphenoid that transmits key mandibular division structures from the middle cranial fossa to the infratemporal fossa.
clinicalNotes: Important skull base landmark for trigeminal anatomy and percutaneous procedures targeting V3-related pathways.

travelsThrough: mandibular division of trigeminal nerve (CN V3)

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the skull base foramen that transmits CN V3.
quizTargets.hint: It is posterolateral to foramen rotundum and anteromedial to foramen spinosum.

structures.description: Canal in the petrous temporal bone that transmits facial, vestibulocochlear, and labyrinthine vascular structures.
clinicalNotes: Commonly evaluated on dedicated IAC MRI for vestibular schwannoma and facial nerve pathology.

quizTargets.prompt: Identify the petrous temporal bone canal carrying CN VII and CN VIII.
quizTargets.hint: It opens into the cerebellopontine angle cistern.
